front: Novel: an American heiress travels through Europe on a new inheritance and rejects suitors Lord Warburton and Caspar Goodwood to marry Gilbert Osmond in Rome
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: Pansy is revealed to be the daughter of Madame Merle
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: Countess Gemini exposes Madame Merle's affair with the protagonist's husband
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: the protagonist visits her dying cousin Ralph Touchett in England, then ambiguously returns to her husband Gilbert Osmond in Rome
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: the protagonist realizes her husband is a complete egotist while reflecting by a dying fireplace in a famous vigil scene
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: preface introduces the celebrated metaphor that 'the house of fiction has in short not one window, but a million'
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: the protagonist first meets her aunt Lydia in the library of her grandmother's house in Albany
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: a Melissa Solomon essay examines the lesbian interests of a character who accuses her ex-lover of having 'dried up my soul'
back: The Portrait of a Lady (Henry James)

front: Novel: a boy named Miles dies in the governess's arms after crying 'Peter Quint — you devil!'
back: The Turn of the Screw (Henry James)

front: Novel: the ghosts of Peter Quint and Miss Jessel haunt the children Miles and Flora at the estate of Bly
back: The Turn of the Screw (Henry James)

front: Novel: the pale-faced, red-haired ghost of Peter Quint causes the housekeeper Mrs. Grose to faint
back: The Turn of the Screw (Henry James)

front: Novel: a boy claims he was expelled from boarding school for merely having 'said things' to people he liked
back: The Turn of the Screw (Henry James)

front: Novel: a boy blows out a woman's candle as they compose a letter
back: The Turn of the Screw (Henry James)

front: Novel: adapted into the 1961 Jack Clayton film The Innocents, co-written by Truman Capote, starring Deborah Kerr as the governess
back: The Turn of the Screw (Henry James)

front: Novel: Daphne Oram was uncredited for her electronic soundscape for an adaptation of this novella
back: The Turn of the Screw (Henry James)

front: Novel: Lambert Strether tells Little Bilham 'Live all you can; it's a mistake not to' in Gloriani's garden in Paris
Novel: a woman desires for her wayward son Chad Newsome to be brought home from Europe by her fiance Lambert Strether
Novel: the protagonist meets Marie de Vionnet after traveling to Paris in pursuit of Chad Newsome
Novel: after recognizing a woman carrying a pink parasol who is riverboating with her lover near a countryside inn, the protagonist comes to doubt the meaning of 'virtuous attachment'
Novel: the preface discusses the benefits and dangers of the first-person narrator and introduces the concept of ficelles, exemplified by Maria Gostrey
Novel: author used increasingly byzantine free indirect discourse to ironic effect in this work following the clueless Lambert Strether
Novel: its plot is reversed in Cynthia Ozick's Foreign Bodies; Ozick also wrote the essay 'What Henry James Knew'
Novel: the sculptor Gloriani, first introduced in Roderick Hudson, owns the Paris garden where the 'live all you can' speech is delivered
Novel: a literary scholar rents a room in a Venetian palazzo from Juliana Bordereau to obtain love letters written to her by the fictional American poet Jeffrey Aspern
Novel: Miss Tita reveals that she burned the title objects at the end of the work
Novel: the narrator pretends to be a gardening enthusiast so he can let a room at an absurdly high price
Novel: the narrator flees to Venice after being called a 'publishing scoundrel'
Novel: preface discusses the pros and cons of writing about 'celebrities who...couldn't possibly have' existed
Novel: a free-spirited American girl from Schenectady visits the Colosseum with the Italian Giovanelli, much to the dismay of Frederick Winterbourne, and dies of Roman fever
Novel: in an opening scene set in Switzerland, the title character remarks on the number of nearby hotels and dismisses complaints about being unable to get 'American candy'
Novel: the feminist orator Verena Tarrant falls in love with the conservative lawyer Basil Ransom, much to his cousin Olive Chancellor's dismay
Novel: ends with the woman's rights orator Verena Tarrant being carried out of a hall with tears 'not the last she was destined to shed' in her eyes
Short Story: John Marcher's conviction that he is fated for a spectacular catastrophe leads him to waste his life and refuse to love May Bartram
back: The Beast in the Jungle (Henry James)

front: Short Story: Eve Kosofsky Sedgwick's essay 'The Beast in the Closet' interprets the protagonist's refusal of a friend's love as repressed homosexuality
back: The Beast in the Jungle (Henry James)

front: Novel: the naive heiress Catherine Sloper is wooed by the gold-digging loafer Morris Townsend against the wishes of her father Dr. Austin Sloper
Novel: despite being set in New York, this novel about Catherine Sloper and Morris Townsend was not included in the New York Edition
Author: introduced the abbreviation 'GAN' for the Great American Novel
Author: essay 'The Art of Fiction' responds to Walter Besant, arguing that novelists should not be confined to writing about their own experiences
Author: his eighteen prefaces to the New York Edition were edited by R.P. Blackmur as The Art of the Novel
Author: Wayne Booth's The Rhetoric of Fiction marks this writer as the starting point for the critical emphasis on 'showing' over 'telling'
Author: one of his prefaces disparages Victorian novels as 'loose, baggy monsters'
Author: Leon Edel wrote a five-volume biography of this writer with volumes subtitled 'The Conquest of London,' 'The Middle Years,' and 'The Treacherous Years'
Author: Philip Guedalla's quip calls this writer's late period 'The Old Pretender'
Author: his stylistic break into the late period followed the depressing failure of his play Guy Domville on stage
Author: Colm Toibin's novel The Master centers on this American author
Author: David Lodge's novel Author, Author is a fictional biography of this writer
Author: after his friend the novelist Constance Woolson committed suicide, he took all her dresses and drowned them in the Venetian lagoon
Author: wrote a homoerotic letter expressing torment at being unable to 'touch you, hold you close and long' to the Norwegian-American sculptor Hendrik Christian Andersen
Author: his book Hawthorne lists 'no Oxford, nor Eton, nor Harrow; no literature, no novels, no museums' among the 'items of high civilization' absent in American life
Author: remarked that an affinity for Poe 'is the mark of a decidedly primitive stage of reflection'
Author: in Alan Hollinghurst's The Line of Beauty, Nick Guest writes a thesis on this writer and adapts The Spoils of Poynton
Author: brother of the pragmatist philosopher William James, author of 'The Will to Believe'
Novel: shows the divorce of Beale and Ida Farange from the perspective of their young daughter
Novel: the bookbinder Hyacinth Robinson shoots himself instead of carrying out an assassination
Novel: said to exemplify 'moral realism' in an essay from Lionel Trilling's The Liberal Imagination
Novel: the title sculptor dies lovelorn after Christina Light marries an Italian prince and later returns as the Princess Casamassima
Novel: a man delivers an ultimatum to his fiancee Kate over an inheritance from the dying Milly Theale, prompting her to declare 'We shall never be again as we were!'
back: The Wings of the Dove (Henry James)

front: Short Story: Hugh Vereker insults a monograph about him as 'twaddle' without realizing its author is sitting next to him
back: The Figure in the Carpet (Henry James)

front: Short Story: after 33 years abroad, Spencer Brydon encounters an alter ego apparition with two missing fingers and a pince-nez in a Manhattan house
Short Story: reverses Maupassant's 'The Necklace'; Arthur Prime lies to Charlotte about jewels in an old tin
Play: on its opening night it was met with hissing from the gallery; an audience member yelled 'It's a bloody good thing y'are!' at a dramatic line
